title: Application of the exact-factorization density-functional perturbation approach to pentacene crystal and monolayer MoS2
authors: Rachel Steinitz-Eliyahu, Galit Cohen, Guy Vosco, E. K. U. Gross, Ryan Requist, Sivan Refaely-Abramson
abstract: Non-adiabatic effects arising from electron-phonon interactions are often neglected within the Born-Oppenheimer (BO) approximation, which assumes that electronic states adjust instantaneously to nuclear motion. The exact factorization (EF) formalism provides a rigorous framework for treating such effects beyond the adiabatic regime and has recently been adapted to density functional theory (DFT) in the harmonic limit. Building on these foundations, we previously introduced an EF-based perturbative scheme, the EF density-functional perturbation theory (EF-DFPT), that enables the computation of phonon-driven non-adiabatic (NA) corrections to Kohn-Sham (KS) electronic states, up to second order in nuclear displacements. Here, we present the first implementation and application of EF-DFPT to extended periodic materials, focusing on its impact on experimentally relevant observables. Using the pentacene molecular crystal and monolayer MoS2 as representative soft- and stiff-mode systems, respectively, we demonstrate how NA electron-phonon interactions modify the static dielectric response. We show that these modifications originate from the combined effect of NA phonon-dressed electronic wavefunctions and second-order NA energy renormalizations. The resulting behavior is strongly material dependent: NA effects are negligible in monolayer MoS2, whereas in pentacene they lead to pronounced long-range screening effects associated with soft vibrational modes and enhanced electron-phonon coupling.
summary: This paper introduces a new perturbative method, EF-DFPT, to calculate non-adiabatic corrections to the electronic properties of solids. It shows that while these corrections are negligible in many materials, they are crucial for describing the enhanced dielectric screening in soft organic crystals like pentacene.
main_problem: The failure of the Born-Oppenheimer approximation to capture non-adiabatic effects arising from electron-phonon interactions in extended periodic materials.
main_result: The study demonstrates that non-adiabatic effects are negligible in stiff-mode systems like monolayer MoS2 but lead to significant long-range screening enhancement in soft-mode systems like pentacene crystals.
method: The implementation of the Exact Factorization Density-Functional Perturbation Theory (EF-DFPT), a perturbative scheme that computes non-adiabatic corrections to Kohn-Sham electronic states up to second order in nuclear displacements.
model_or_system: The study applies the EF-DFPT framework to two contrasting periodic systems: the pentacene molecular crystal (a soft-mode system with strong electron-phonon coupling) and monolayer MoS2 (a stiff-mode transition metal dichalcogenide).
key_observables: Static dielectric response (inverse dielectric function), electronic bandstructure energies, and wavefunction overlap matrix elements.
important_parameters: Soft-mode vs. stiff-mode regimes, zero-temperature limit (zero-point motion), and long-range interaction regime (small q).
main_assumptions: The implementation uses the harmonic limit for nuclear motion and the rigid-ion approximation for computing energy corrections.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows crystal structures, adiabatic vs. non-adiabatic potentials, and wavefunction overlap; Figure 2 displays renormalized bandstructures and energy corrections; Figure 3 compares the inverse dielectric function for pentacene.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from the theoretical derivation of the EF-DFPT framework to its first implementation in periodic solids, followed by comparative applications to pentacene and MoS2 to demonstrate material-dependent non-adiabatic effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Fig. 1(a,b) presents the structures of pentacene and MoS2, the characteristic systems examined in this work (for full computational details, see SI). Fig. 1(c) shows the computed average values of the adiabatic and NA perturbations, as given in equations (2),(3), respectively, for the pentacene crystal and MoS2 (further details are given in Fig. S2 in the SI). While in both materials the NA corrections are small, as expected, in pentacene they are about two orders of magnitude smaller than the adi- abatic correction, while in MoS2 they are three orders of magnitude smaller and are practically zero.
